content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sncramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fnx (916) 322-0886 
Larissa Seto 
Assistant City Attorney 
123 Main Street 
P. 0. Box 520 
Pleasanton, CA 94566 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-241 
Dear Ms. Seto: 
October 18, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Pleasanton Planning 
Commissioner Nancy Allen regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act 
(the "Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit Commissioner Allen from taking part in a governmental decision 
concerning the rebuild of a Kentucky Fried Chicken ("KFC") when it comes before the Planning 
Commission? 
CONCLUSION 
No. The Act does not prohibit Commissioner Allen from taking part in the decision. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-17-241 
Page No. 2 
The Planning Commission will be considering a proposal by Kentucky Fried Chicken 
("KFC") to demolish and rebuild their restaurant at 1803 Santa Rita Road in Pleasanton. The 
existing KFC drive-thru restaurant is 2,132 square feet in size, and the proposed replacement drive­
thru restaurant will be 2,479 square feet in size. 
Commissioner Allen owns and lives in a single-family home in Pleasanton. Her interest in 
that property is greater than $2,000. Commissioner Allen's home is part of the Danbury Park 
Homeowners' Association. The Association owns the private streets known as Trimingham Drive, 
Oxsen Street, Groth Court, and Elliott Circle. There are about 200 single family homes that form 
the Association. There are no Association common recreational facilities, just the previously listed 
private streets, a few fences adjacent to those private streets, a common sound wall around part of 
the perimeter and landscaping near the entranceway. 
The western most portion of the Association's Elliott Circle is located about 380 feet from 
the KFC site. Commissioner Allen's home is about 1,275 feet from the KFC site. Commissioner 
Allen cannot see, hear or smell the KFC site from her home. Portions of the KFC site may be 
visible while on the western portion of Elliott Circle, but the intervening City park and six lane 
Santa Rita Road create visual and physical separation. Neither the Association's private roads, nor 
Commissioner Allen's home, have direct access to the KFC site. They are separated by one to two 
arterial streets and private streets. The KFC rebuild project will not change the zoning or uses 
allowed on the Association's private streets, nor for Commissioner Allen's home. 
ANALYSIS 
Under Section 87100, the general rule is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public 
official makes, participates in making, or uses his or her positon to influence a governmental 
decision which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the 
official's financial interests. 
You have identified one interest that may result in a conflict of interest for Commissioner 
Allen- a real property interest in her single-family home.2 
Foreseeability 
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. The standard for foreseeability 
differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. Under the facts you 
have provided, Commissioner Allen's property is not explicitly involved in the decision. Thus, as 
applied to her property, Regulation 1870 I (b) provides: "In general, if the financial effect can be 
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably 
foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not 
subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable." 
2 Regulation 18702.2(d)(4) provides: Real property in which an official has a financial interest does not 
include any common area as part of the official's ownership interest in a common interest development as defined in the 
Davis-Stirling Common Interest Development Act (Civil Code, Sections 4000 et seq.). 


Materiality 
File No. A-17-241 
Page No. 3 
As relevant to your facts, the financial effect of the Planning Commission decision on 
Commissioner Allen's real property is material if the decision: 
• 
Would change the character of the parcel ofreal property by substantially altering traffic 
levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the official's real 
property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other 
factors that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in which the official 
has a financial interest (Regulation 18702.2( a)( 10). ); 
• 
Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the 
circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its 
reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's property 
(Regulation 18702.2(a)(l 2).) 
You state that the KFC site is currently occupied by an existing KFC drive-thru restaurant, 
and that Commissioner Allen cannot see, hear or smell the KFC site from her home. The area 
immediately surrounding it is highly developed, characterized by wide streets with high traffic 
volumes, residential housing developments, commercial or retail businesses, offices, and a park. 
The facts presented do not provide any evidence that that the K.FC proposal will have any impact on 
Commissioner Allen's parking, views, privacy, noise levels, or air quality at her residence, or that 
the intensity of use of properties in her single-family residential neighborhood will be altered by the 
decision. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Jack Woodside 
General Counsel 
22 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit Commissioner Allen from taking part in a governmental decision 
concerning the rebuild of a Kentucky Fried Chicken ("KFC") when it comes before the Planning 
Commission?
sections.conclusion: No. The Act does not prohibit Commissioner Allen from taking part in the decision.

concerning the rebuild of a Kentucky Fried Chicken ("KFC") when it comes before the Planning 
Commission?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit Commissioner Allen from taking part in the decision.
